glossary.def: The continuous-time limit of a random walk: a path that starts at zero, has independent Gaussian increments with variance equal to the elapsed time, and is continuous everywhere yet nowhere differentiable. Its variance is exactly the clock, Var(W_t) = t.

A process whose expected future value given all past information equals its present value, E[M_{t+s} | F_t] = M_t. It is the formal model of a fair game; under the risk-neutral measure a discounted asset price is a martingale, which is why options price as discounted expectations.

The limit of the sum of squared increments of a path. For a smooth curve it is zero, but for Brownian motion it equals t, giving the rule (dW)^2 = dt — the single fact that makes stochastic calculus differ from ordinary calculus.

The standard stock model, dS = mu*S dt + sigma*S dW, in which randomness acts on returns rather than the price level. Its solution makes prices lognormal (never negative) and its log drifts at mu − ½σ² rather than mu.

The chain rule of stochastic calculus. For a function of an Itô process it keeps an extra second-order term, ½σ²·∂²f/∂x², that ordinary calculus discards — the term born from quadratic variation that produces every ½σ² correction in quantitative finance.

The ½σ² gap by which the log-drift of GBM, mu − ½σ², falls below the arithmetic drift mu. It is why a volatile asset with positive expected return can have a typical (median) path that still loses money: the mean is pulled up by the lognormal's fat right tail while the median lags.

An equation dX = a(X,t) dt + b(X,t) dW expressing a process's infinitesimal change as a deterministic drift plus a random diffusion. Choosing a and b selects the model; most SDEs are solved numerically by the Euler–Maruyama scheme.

A mean-reverting SDE, dX = kappa(theta − X) dt + sigma dW, pulled back toward a long-run level theta at speed kappa. Unlike Brownian motion its variance saturates at σ²/2κ, making it the canonical model for rates, spreads, and mean-reverting pairs.

refs.cite: Itô, K. (1944). Stochastic Integral. Proc. Imperial Acad. Tokyo 20(8).
Bachelier, L. (1900). Théorie de la spéculation. Ann. Sci. ÉNS 17.
Øksendal, B. (2003). Stochastic Differential Equations: An Introduction with Applications (6th ed.). Springer.
Uhlenbeck, G. E. & Ornstein, L. S. (1930). On the Theory of the Brownian Motion. Phys. Rev. 36.
Einstein, A. (1905). Über die von der molekularkinetischen Theorie der Wärme geforderte Bewegung von in ruhenden Flüssigkeiten suspendierten Teilchen. Ann. Phys. 322.
Wiener, N. (1923). Differential Space. J. Math. Phys. 2.
